skill.name: workflow-guide
skill.description: Explains a two-agent workflow where Cursor acts as project manager and Claude Code acts as implementation worker.
skill.summary: Guide teams through Cursor and Claude Code collaboration workflows.
skill.author: Chachamaru127

security_audit.summary: Static findings were reviewed as false positives caused by Markdown formatting, slash-command examples, and multilingual documentation. No executable scripts, network calls, secret access, prompt injection, or malicious intent were found in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: Markdown Slash Commands Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The reported command execution locations are Markdown examples and slash-command references for a workflow guide. No shell interpreter, Ruby backtick execution, or user-controlled command construction is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations contain fenced Markdown blocks, inline marker names, and documented slash commands. The skill frontmatter allows only Read, and no executable source file or command invocation API exists.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alert Is a Metadata False Positive
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The reported line is the skill description metadata and mentions Claude Code workflow terms. No cryptographic function, hashing algorithm, credential handling, or security-sensitive implementation is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact line is descriptive frontmatter for workflow routing. It does not contain crypto code or an algorithm reference that would create a real weak-cryptography risk.

low_findings.title: Entropy and Reconnaissance Alerts Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The entropy heuristic is explained by Japanese text, emoji, tables, and Markdown examples. The reconnaissance alert refers to an example login error message, not host or environment discovery.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed files are plain Markdown documentation with no encoded payload or system collection logic. The cited error text is part of a bug-fix scenario example.

content.user_title: Coordinate Cursor and Claude Code Workflows
content.value_statement: Teams often lose context when planning and implementation happen in separate tools. This skill explains a clear Cursor and Claude Code workflow with task markers, handoffs, and review steps.
actual_capabilities: Explains Cursor as project manager and Claude Code as implementation worker.
Defines Plans.md task markers for requested, active, completed, and reviewed work.
Lists workflow commands used for planning, work execution, status sync, and handoff.
Shows typical feature, bug fix, CI failure, and parallel task flows.
Describes escalation when automated CI fixes fail three times.
limitations: Provides documentation and guidance only; it does not run automation.
Assumes the project uses Plans.md and compatible workflow commands.
Does not validate deployments, test results, or production readiness.
Does not replace human review for release decisions.

use_cases.title: Onboard a New Developer
use_cases.description: Explain how task requests, implementation, completion reports, and review approvals move between Cursor and Claude Code.

use_cases.title: Standardize AI Handoffs
use_cases.description: Create consistent task markers and completion reports so project status remains clear across AI-assisted sessions.

use_cases.title: Improve Review Flow
use_cases.description: Use the guide to separate implementation work from review and production deployment decisions.

prompt_templates.title: Explain the Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain the Cursor and Claude Code two-agent workflow in simple steps for a new team member.

prompt_templates.title: Map My Task Status
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose the correct Plans.md status markers for my current task list.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Handoff
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a clear handoff plan from Cursor to Claude Code for this feature request.

prompt_templates.title: Review the Process
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit our Cursor and Claude Code workflow for unclear ownership, missing review steps, and risky deployment decisions.

output_examples.input: Explain who owns each step in the workflow.
output_examples.output: Cursor owns task assignment, review, and production deployment approval. Claude Code owns implementation, tests, CI fixes, and completion reporting.

output_examples.input: What should happen after Claude Code finishes a task?
output_examples.output: Claude Code prepares a completion report. Cursor reviews the changes, checks staging results, and decides whether more work is needed.

output_examples.input: How should repeated CI failures be handled?
output_examples.output: Stop automatic fixes after three failed attempts. Summarize attempted fixes, likely causes, and recommended next actions for review.

best_practices: Keep task markers current before asking another agent to continue work.
Use completion reports that include changed files, tests, and next actions.
Escalate production deployment decisions to a human reviewer.
anti_patterns: Treating Claude Code completion as automatic production approval.
Starting implementation before the task request is clear.
Continuing repeated CI fixes without escalation or review.

faq.question: What does this skill help explain?
faq.answer: It explains how Cursor and Claude Code can divide planning, implementation, review, and deployment responsibilities.

faq.question: Does this skill execute workflow commands?
faq.answer: No. It only provides documentation and guidance about the workflow.

faq.question: Can I use it without Cursor?
faq.answer: Yes. You can still use the task markers and review steps for solo work.

faq.question: What is Plans.md used for?
faq.answer: Plans.md tracks task status, ownership, and review progress across the workflow.

faq.question: Who should approve production deployment?
faq.answer: A human reviewer or project manager should approve production deployment after reviewing the work.

faq.question: Is this skill safe for published marketplace use?
faq.answer: Yes. The reviewed files are documentation and do not contain executable code or confirmed dangerous behavior.
